title: Building a REST API with Node.js and Express: A Step-by-Step Guide

hero.tagline: From zero to a working API in under an hour
hero.caption: Setting up your development environment

blocks.text: This tutorial assumes you have **Node.js v18+** and **npm** installed. You can verify with `node --version` and `npm --version`.

blocks.content: Before we begin, make sure you have a terminal open and a fresh project directory ready. All of the commands in this guide are cross-platform and will work on macOS, Linux, and Windows.

items: Postman or curl for testing endpoints

blocks.content: Open your terminal and create a new directory for the project. Then initialize it with npm and install the required dependencies.

blocks.code:
mkdir my-rest-api
cd my-rest-api
npm init -y
npm install express cors dotenv

blocks.text: Always use `npm init -y` for quick scaffolding. You can edit the generated `package.json` file later to add metadata like author, description, and license.
Step 2: Create the Server Entry Point

blocks.content: Create a file called `index.js` in your project root. This will be the main entry point for your application. We will start with a basic server setup.
The code above sets up a basic Express server with CORS enabled and JSON body parsing. The dotenv package loads environment variables from a `.env` file, which we will create next.

blocks.text: Never commit your `.env` file to version control. Add it to `.gitignore` immediately. Environment files may contain API keys, database credentials, and other secrets.

blocks.content: Now we will create a simple CRUD interface for a collection of items. Create a `routes` directory and add a `items.js` file. This keeps your code organized and follows the separation of concerns principle.

This router handles three endpoints: a GET to list all items, a GET with an ID to retrieve a single item, and a POST to create a new item. Each handler returns structured JSON responses with appropriate HTTP status codes.

blocks.text: Use **HTTP status codes** correctly: `200` for success, `201` for created resources, `404` for not found, and `500` for server errors. Consistent status codes make your API easier to consume.

blocks.content: Update your main `index.js` file to use the items router. This is where all the pieces come together into a working application.
Start the server with `node index.js` and open a new terminal window. Use curl to test each endpoint and verify that everything works as expected.

blocks.code:
# Start the server
node index.js

# In a separate terminal, test the endpoints
curl http://localhost:3000/api/items
curl http://localhost:3000/api/items/1
curl -X POST http://localhost:3000/api/items \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"name":"Monitor","price":350}'

blocks.text: If you see a blank response or connection refused, double-check that the server is running on the correct port and that no other service is using port 3000.

blocks.content: Congratulations! You have built a fully functional REST API. From here, you can add a database connection, implement authentication, add input validation, or deploy to a cloud provider. The foundation is solid.
